title: Modals of deduction
explanation:
Modals of deduction

Use must for strong logical conclusions, might / may / could for possibility, and can't for negative deduction.

questions.text: She has worked all night. She [*must be|can't be|might be] exhausted.
He is not answering. He [*might be|must be|can't be] in a meeting.
That [*can't be|must be|might be] James - he is on holiday in Spain.
The lights are off, but someone is inside. They [*must have gone|can't have gone|might have been going] to sleep early.
I am not sure where Anna is. She [*may have missed|must have missed|can't have missed] the train.

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: You have been travelling for twenty hours. You ____ tired. | *must be | can't be | might not
The shop is closed already, so they ____ gone home. | *must have | can't have | might not have
He knows nothing about the plan, so he ____ seen the email. | *can't have | must have | may have
I cannot find my keys - they ____ be in the kitchen. | *might | must | can't
They [*must have forgotten|can't have forgotten|may not] about the appointment because nobody came.
This [*can't be|must be|might be] the right address - the number is wrong.
She looks delighted. She [*must have passed|might have passed|can't have passed] the exam.
Do not worry. There [*could be|must be|can't be] a simple explanation.
He sounds ill. He [*may need|can't need|mustn't need] to see a doctor.
